flashcards.front: Define Relational Algebra and list its fundamental operations.
flashcards.back: Relational Algebra is a procedural query language that uses operators to transform relations (tables) to obtain desired results. The fundamental operations are: Selection (σ), Projection (π), Union (∪), Set Difference (−), Cartesian Product (×), and Rename (ρ).

flashcards.front: Given tables Employee(EID, Name, DeptID, Salary) and Department(DeptID, DeptName), write a RA query to find names of employees earning > 50000 using Selection and Projection.
flashcards.back: π_Name(σ_Salary>50000(Employee)) — The Selection operator (σ) filters rows where Salary > 50000, then Projection (π) extracts only the Name column. Equivalent SQL: SELECT Name FROM Employee WHERE Salary > 50000;

flashcards.front: Explain the Natural Join (⋈) operation with a practical example involving Student and Enrollment tables.
flashcards.back: Natural Join combines two relations based on common attributes with the same name, automatically equating all shared attributes. Example: Student(SID, Name, City) ⋈ Enrollment(SID, CID) produces result with attributes (SID, Name, City, CID), matching on SID. SQL: SELECT * FROM Student NATURAL JOIN Enrollment;

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Equijoin (Theta Join) and Natural Join? Provide RA notation.
flashcards.back: Equijoin (or Theta Join with =) explicitly specifies the join condition using subscript: R ⋈_{condition} S (e.g., R ⋈_{R.B=S.B} S). Natural Join automatically joins on all common attributes without explicit condition. Equijoin may produce duplicate columns; Natural Join eliminates one duplicate column.

flashcards.front: Using set intersection (∩), write a RA expression to find students enrolled in both DBMS and AI courses from tables EnrolledDBMS(SID) and EnrolledAI(SID).
flashcards.back: EnrolledDBMS ∩ EnrolledAI — This returns SID values present in both relations. Set operations require compatible schemas (same attributes in same order). SQL equivalent: SELECT SID FROM EnrolledDBMS INTERSECT SELECT SID FROM EnrolledAI;

flashcards.front: Explain the Division operator (÷) with a scenario: Find all suppliers who supply all parts in the Parts table using Supplier(SupID, PartID) and Parts(PartID).
flashcards.back: Supplier ÷ Parts — Returns SupID of suppliers who have a tuple for EVERY PartID in Parts. Conceptually: {s | ∀p ∈ Parts, (s,p) ∈ Supplier}. Used for 'all' and 'every' queries. SQL requires subqueries: SELECT DISTINCT SupID FROM Supplier s WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT PartID FROM Parts WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Supplier WHERE SupID=s.SupID AND PartID=Parts.PartID));

flashcards.front: Given R(A,B) and S(B,C), show the Cartesian Product followed by Selection on B to perform an Equijoin on B, and simplify.
flashcards.back: σ_{R.B=S.B}(R × S) — This is equivalent to R ⋈_{R.B=S.B} S (Theta Join). The Cartesian Product generates all |R|×|S| combinations, then Selection filters only tuples where B matches. This demonstrates how derived operator 'Join' can be expressed using fundamental operations.

flashcards.front: Convert this SQL query to Relational Algebra: SELECT P.Name FROM Product P, Category C WHERE P.CatID=C.CatID AND C.Type='Electronics';
flashcards.back: π_Name(σ_Type='Electronics'(Product ⋈_{Product.CatID=Category.CatID} Category)) — First perform Equijoin on CatID, then Selection filters Type='Electronics', finally Projection extracts Name. Alternative using fundamental ops: π_Name(σ_{Product.CatID=Category.CatID}(σ_Type='Electronics'(Category) × Product))
